title: Random entanglement percolation on realistic quantum networks
abstract: We study random entanglement percolation in heterogeneous quantum networks, where the singlet-conversion probabilities (SCPs) of the edges are drawn from a probability distribution rather than being fixed. After briefly recalling random classical and random quantum entanglement percolation, we focus on polarization-dependent loss (PDL) as a physical source of random edge entanglement in photonic networks. In this setting, polarization imbalance induces a simple map from the PDL magnitude to the edge SCP. We illustrate this map for representative PDL models and discuss the resulting implications for entanglement percolation.
summary: This paper explores how random fluctuations in edge entanglement affect the ability to establish long-range connectivity in quantum networks. By focusing on polarization-dependent loss in photonic networks, the author shows that the distribution of entanglement quality matters significantly for quantum protocols. Specifically, the 'quantum penalty' in RQEP makes the network performance sensitive to the entire probability distribution of the edges, not just the average. This provides a more realistic framework for designing robust quantum communication infrastructures.
main_problem: Investigating how heterogeneity in edge entanglement, specifically caused by polarization-dependent loss (PDL), affects entanglement percolation in quantum networks.
main_result: The study demonstrates that while Random Classical Entanglement Percolation (RCEP) depends only on the mean singlet-conversion probability, Random Quantum Entanglement Percolation (RQEP) depends on the full distribution of probabilities due to the quantum penalty of the q-swap protocol.
method: The author uses a mathematical mapping between PDL magnitude and singlet-conversion probabilities (SCP) and analyzes the percolation thresholds using RCEP and RQEP frameworks with q-swap preprocessing.
model_or_system: A photonic quantum network where qubits are encoded in orthogonal polarization modes and edges are subject to random singlet-conversion probabilities (SCPs) induced by polarization-dependent loss (PDL).
key_observables: Singlet-conversion probability (SCP), PDL magnitude (in dB), and the quantum penalty constant (C).
important_parameters: PDL magnitude (P), mean SCP (mu), standard deviation (sigma), and the weak-PDL regime.
main_assumptions: The study focuses specifically on PDL as a source of randomness and assumes the use of the q-swap preprocessing protocol.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the probability density of PDL for three different models (left) and the resulting induced SCP densities (right).
paper_structure: The paper begins by recalling classical and quantum entanglement percolation, introduces the PDL-induced SCP model, derives the mapping between PDL and SCP, analyzes the RCEP and RQEP regimes, and concludes with specific examples of induced distributions from real-world PDL models.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete physical link between realistic fiber-optic impairments (PDL) and the fundamental limits of entanglement distribution in quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. – Left: probability density of the polarization-dependent loss P. Right: corresponding induced SCP density obtained from Eq. (4). The Mecozzi–Shtaif and Galtarossa–Palmieri curves were matched to the same mean PDL, ⟨P⟩≈2.35 dB, while the Lin–Jiang model used the ﬁve- element setting (0.8, 1.2, 1.4, 1.0, 0.7) dB.
